data.title: Wynn Resorts: Betting On UAE
data.description:
Summary

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) is facing renewed competition within Macau, which could hurt margins. In addition, its highly anticipated UAE integrated resort, Al Marjan, which is expected to come online in 1H27, may not be a significant game-changing EBITDA driver for the company as a whole. We believe the share price is fairly valued at these levels. Forward EBITDA growth estimates are only at +1.6% vs. +5% for the sector average.

Wynn Resorts: global gaming operator

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) is a leading integrated gaming resort operator with two properties in Macau, two in Las Vegas, and one in Boston. Wynn's gaming properties are aimed at the higher-spending premium player in terms of providing luxurious hotel amenities coupled with high-end retail and food/beverage offerings. As a percentage of EBITDAR in 2025, Wynn's USA operations comprised 51% of the group's total compared to 50% of the total in 2024.

2025 saw EBITDAR fall in Macau and Las Vegas, mainly due to lower casino hold percentages, meaning players were lucky and won at a higher than typical theoretical rate. Management guided on the 4Q25 earnings call that Las Vegas EBITDAR would have been up flat to up low single digits yoy if normalizing these theoretical rates. Overall, I suspect that Wynn's gaming and hotel businesses remain healthy with positive volume growth.

EBITDAR USD '000 2024 2025 yoy
Wynn Macau 441,852 402,125 -9%
Wynn Palace 733,710 682,900 -7%
Total Macau 1,175,562 1,085,025 -8%
Las Vegas 946,762 902,405 -5%
Boston 247,128 236,721 -4%
Total USA 1,193,890 1,139,126 -5%
Total 2,369,452 2,224,151 -6%

LVS issued a warning sign recently

The premium gaming segment is the fastest growing in Macau, with all six gaming operators targeting the same class of higher spending customer. This results in margin pressure and lower earnings for the gaming operators. On April 23, LVS released earnings and specifically mentioned that the premium gaming segment remains competitive in Macau and could result in margin compression. LVS EBITDA margins in Macau fell by 1.4% pts yoy, and the stock fell by 8% in after-market trading.

Intense competition expected

Wynn opened a premium gaming area called Chairman's Club at Wynn Palace in February 2026. Chairman's Club expanded by 3x in terms of area to now be at 100,000 square feet and features additional food/beverage amenities for the higher end player. This new area resulted in strong gaming volumes and likely gaming market share gains, as evidenced by a recent report from Citi.

Our concern is that new capacity from Macau competitors will be introduced later in the year, all targeting the same premium player segment. MLCO will open its rebranded REM hotel in 2H26, formerly the Countdown Hotel, which has been closed since 2021 for renovation. REM will feature 150 suites with an average size of 1,000 square feet and have an avant-garde design. MGM Cotai will launch 124 premium suites in April. Galaxy Entertainment officially opened the luxury Capella Hotel in February. LVS recently said that new suites at its flagship Venetian property will open later in 2026.

Given that all of these properties (especially MGM and Melco) are within close proximity, Wynn could see market share loss as 2026 progresses. MGM, Melco, and Wynn's properties on Cotai are located adjacent to one another, which takes less than 5 minutes to walk among the properties. To combat market share loss, gaming operators could continue to engage in competitive marketing to attract players, which could place further pressure on EBITDA margins and hurt profitability.

Gaming growth is slowing in Macau

Total GGR (gross gaming revenue) for Macau grew by 9% in 2024 vs. 24% in 2023. YTD through March, GGR is +14%. Current consensus estimates for GGR growth in 2026 are around mid-single digits. In 2025, Macau's GGR per visitor fell by 5% yoy. If gaming growth is moderate this year, this means that the entire gaming market is not expanding significantly, which could place further pressure on operators to chase a limited premium customer base.

Moderate sector revenue growth is due to geopolitical risks leading to greater consumer uncertainty and slower economic growth from rising oil prices. In addition, prolonged economic slowdown in China, where the Chinese government forecasted GDP growth of 4.5% vs. 5% in 2025, coupled with a weak property market, slowing consumer consumption, and tariff uncertainty, is likely to rein in consumer spending in 2026, which could further hurt gaming revenues.

US operations are likely to be steady in 2026

We do not expect significant upside from Wynn's US gaming operations, as gaming volumes are likely to be flattish +/- a few percentage points. The US gaming market is relatively mature. Year-to-date through February, visitors to Las Vegas are flattish yoy, with convention visitors +3% yoy. Overall, Las Vegas hotel occupancy is down around 1% yoy, with REVPAR (revenue per available room) +4% yoy. Reasons for slower Las Vegas visitation include fewer international tourists plus weaker consumer confidence.

Not much upside from Wynn Al Marjan Island

Wynn has a 40% equity stake in Wynn Al Marjan Island, which will be the first gaming-integrated resort to open in the Middle East, with an expected opening in Spring 2027. The resort will feature around 1,500 rooms, in addition to a theater, 22 food outlets, shopping, and a beach club. The property is about a 50-minute drive from Dubai Airport.

Wynn is also planning a second integrated resort in the UAE on Al Marjan Island to be completed by late 2028. However, this property will be smaller in scale with only 132 rooms. Wynn management noted its UAE property could result in $465 million total in terms of EBITDA, which already takes into account the return of expats into the region following recent geopolitical events coupled with strong tourism growth (+5% yoy or 19.6m visitors to UAE in 2025).

We estimate the $465m guidance to be around 20% of Wynn Resorts total EBITDA. While I am positive on the earnings contribution, the 20% share of EBITDA is far less than MGM Osaka, which could potentially increase MGM Resort's total EBITDA by nearly 2x based on my view. The new property effect on earnings may not cause an MGM stock re-rating, but not Wynn.

Lack of catalysts - Hold ratings

Wynn will report Q1 2026 earnings in early May, where we expect strong volumes from Macau but will watch the EBITDA margins, which we believe could come under pressure due to intense competition. Given that no new gaming or hotel capacity at Wynn will come online in the near term along with margin concerns, we do not expect significant EBITDA growth for Wynn and believe the share price is fairly valued at current levels and maintain a HOLD rating. Consensus estimates for Wynn's forward EBITDA growth are only +1.6% vs. sector median at 5%.

Risks

Upside risks to our view are a stabilizing player marketing spend environment in Macau or stronger-than-expected market share gains in Macau. In addition, a significant recovery in Las Vegas gaming and hotel operations would lead to upside risks for the share price.

Conclusion

My main concern with the Wynn stock is the lack of clear earnings upside for 2026 (less than 2% EBITDA growth) given a stagnant US gaming market and a slowing Macau gaming market. These facts, coupled with the potential for lower margins from increased competition in Macau, along with Wynn Al Marjan not being a large percentage of future group EBITDA, result in our view that the share price could stay range-bound.

data.title: QQA: How Equity-Linked Notes Can Complement A Core Technology Holding
data.description:
The Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF (QQA) is a passive index-fund-like ETF that offers top technology equity or ETF exposure, coupled with ELNs or equity-linked notes and a liquidity reserve that allows the fund managers to employ derivatives leverage as they deem fit for whatever the current market situation might be.

Here I'll explain how it's constructed, what its derivative layers enhance or affect in terms of performance (and how they do so), and what type of investor it's best geared for. The endgame is to give you as much meaningful information as possible so you can make more informed decisions regarding potential use of this ETF. So let's get started.

Construction

The ETF tracks the NASDAQ-100 Index [NDX], and uses a "full replication" method to make picks for its portfolio, which means whatever funds are earmarked for stock purchases are allocated using the index weightings, so it's nearly identical to the Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ)'s composition. There's a skew from the cash equivalents and ELN holdings, which I'll get to in a minute, but otherwise the holdings are just a mirror of QQQ.

That's the first part of this ETF, and it accounts for the majority of holdings. Then come the cash reserves in the form of liquid assets like money market fund investments or holding treasury obligations. This could be as much as 20% of total assets, and it serves to provide immediate liquidity in a crisis situation and adds interest income to the mix.

The equity and cash buffer holdings don't offer any leverage, obviously, so that's achieved by a third ingredient in the form of equity-linked notes, or ELNs. Let's understand this instrument before we move ahead with our study of the ETF, since it's the part that generates the high yield (10% trailing).

The Role of Equity-Linked Notes

ELNs are combinations of debt and leverage, the leverage part comprising an underlying options play. The "coupon" on the debt you're buying gives you some amount of downside protection in the form of the high yield, and you also benefit from a capped equity upside.

QQA's ELNs are structured as a way to offshore the options strategy, letting the ELN's issuing bank handle it. That's how QQA is able to sell itself as a passive fund with an ER of 0.29% (net of a 0.04% indefinite waiver).

ELNs are custom products, so there are specific strikes and other parameters for the underlying calls and cash-secured puts that won't match up with a different one, such as what the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) uses.

JEPQ's ELN-embedded calls are sold at or very near the money, so they require the fund managers to take an active approach, as opposed to QQA's passive strategy that uses out-of-the-money options. The premiums are lower with QQA, which is why you'll see JEPQ with a higher yield (10.5% trailing). That said, QQA does better than JEPQ on the ER front (cheaper) as well as on the TR front (yield + capital gains).

I'm not telling you that JEPQ is worse than QQA, because they suit two different priorities for income investors—either leverage more aggressively for higher income or stay passive and try to grab more upward momentum off the equity part. JEPQ investors are likely to be more income-oriented and less concerned about capital appreciation. On the other hand, QQA offers a lower but comparable yield, but with better exposure to long-term capital gains. This is reflected by their investment objectives: JEPQ's main goal is to "seek current income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation" while QQA has a slightly different objective, which is to "seek total return through current income and long-term growth of capital." The Fund seeks current income while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation.

What Can Move Markets

As of early April 2026 (the time of writing), the macro picture's playing out like it did last year after the Liberation Day tariffs first rattled the markets. Another bull rally has begun after a mixed year of growth and stagnation. In such an environment, investors who want a somewhat more cautious, income-forward way to participate in what may be flatter markets may choose QQA.

The S&P (SP500) lost 4.3% in Q1-26, even more than what was rotated out of European and Asian markets (ex-Japan). All this money came flowing back when the market went risk-on in April, and month-to-date gains have been so strong that QQA and JEPQ both missed out on a big chunk of upside. This illustrates the effect of structurally capped upside participation in this sort of ETF.

That's the hurdle I'm talking about. You're getting a high yield but at great cost, which means these funds aren't going to get you alpha on a TR basis as long as the market stays strong.

Funds like QQA and JEPQ do best when the market moves sideways, and these funds generally do better than pure equities during declines.

If you noticed that JEPQ protected capital better than QQA, you're right. That's the protection you're getting from the high yield. In the November to March timeframe, when the market was moving sideways but gradually down, the high volatility of the NDX index would have boosted premiums in both ETFs, but since JEPQ's options have lower moneyness, they benefit more. The trade-off is the active management and the higher ER.

The Right Fit

QQA could theoretically work for any income investor, so the right fit is a question of how much you want as income, how much upside you're willing to give up, and how much protection you're looking for.

The ETF holds quite a bit of liquid assets "to provide additional downside protection by limiting the Fund’s exposure to equity market risk." Read that as 'we keep a bunch of cash that earns short-term rates and further protects your assets.' Downside is always unpredictable, but you've got options premiums and interest income, both helping to offset any declines.

The best use case for this ETF is as an income engine that's complementary to a core equities portfolio. QQA will provide the additional equity base required to run the options strategy, and the options embedded in the ELNs will juice those securities to give you a monthly paycheck. Your main block of equities can then run free and capture full upside, and they get marginal downside protection from QQA.

This article answers three main questions about QQA:

  1. What is QQA's objective and how do ELNs play a role in its aim to achieve it?
  2. In which investment environments does QQA perform best?
  3. Which investors would most appreciate QQA and how can it fit in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF. data.title: To A Trillion(s) Dollars And Beyond: A SpaceX IPO Odyssey
data.description:
In 2001: A Space Odyssey, a movie that was well ahead of its time when it was released in 1968, Hal (the computer) famously responded to questions about his reliability with "it (mistakes) can only be attributable to human error”. I was reminded of my fallibility repeatedly as I tried to value SpaceX (SPACE) ahead of its initial public offering, a market debut that is shaping up as a barn-burner for three reasons. The first is that in a market where there are many young companies all trying to claim to be futuristic in their offerings, SpaceX clearly stands out as the real thing, with rockets, satellites and AI all residing under its corporate umbrella. The second is that its founder (Elon Musk) is the richest person in the world, has upended one legacy business (autos), bought a social media company as a soapbox and made his presence felt on the political stage. Love him or hate him, Musk is definitely not boring, and his capacity to spin business narratives that seem outlandish at first hearing but become conventional wisdom later clearly adds to the allure of SpaceX. Third, if the private market pricing feeds into the public offering, SpaceX could very well become the most valuable IPO of all time, joining the rarefied list of trillion-dollar companies on listing. That said, I may be getting a little ahead of the game here, because SpaceX has not filed a public prospectus yet, and little is known about its financials other than drabs of information that have been leaked to the press. I will forge on, nevertheless, with the stipulation that this is a first iteration and that I will revisit it as more information comes out about the firm’s financial standing and its IPO plans.

The History of SpaceX

You may be surprised to hear that SpaceX is older than Tesla, at least in terms of chronological age, founded on March 14, 2002, in El Segundo, California. At its founding, Musk stated its goals as reducing the costs of space transportation and travel to Mars but was viewed as having little chance of success by the space establishment, composed then of government agencies (NASA) and a few defense firms (Boeing and Northrop Grumman). SpaceX applied the lessons of modular engineering from the software business, and it launched Falcon 1, its first space launch vehicle, in September 2008; that successful launch led to a NASA contract for $1.6 billion and rescued the company from near bankruptcy. In subsequent years, SpaceX developed Falcon 9, a reusable and heavier vehicle, with the Dragon Spacecraft unit, and became the first commercial entity to deliver cargo to the International Space Station. In 2013, SpaceX launched its first mission for a private customer and quickly secured a dominant market share of commercial launch contract market. In recent years, SpaceX has invested in an even more ambitious version (in terms of size and power) of reusable spacecraft with Starship, and while its first launch in 2023 exploded in space, the company is clearly moving towards making it functional.

Along the way, the company added to its business mix, first with The Boring Company, a company that specialized in building tunnels that could be used to transport people, in 2017, before spinning it off as a separate entity. More significantly, in 2019, the company launched sixty Starlink satellites, with the end game of offering satellite-based internet services to customers, especially in areas where conventional internet service was limited. That endeavor has now grown to include thousands of satellites and had more than ten million active subscribers spread across the world at the end of 2025. In February 2026, the company created its third business arm with its acquisition of xAI, the parent of Grok, the Musk-developed competitor in the LLM space.

SpaceX had a slow start financially, as its initial years were spent developing the Falcon 1 rocket, and even after that development, the dependence on the US government and commercial satellite launchers resulted in revenues growing much more slowly than they did at Tesla, Musk's other high-profile creation. Even as late as 2021, SpaceX reported revenues of just over $2 billion, almost entirely from its launch business, but Starlink's subscriber-based model has allowed revenues to increase more than five-fold since, reaching an estimated $15.6 billion in 2025, with just under 30% coming from the launch business ($4.1 billion) and about the rest from Starlink subscriptions and related businesses ($11.4 billion); xAI, which was acquired in 2026, had subscription revenues of roughly $100 million in 2025. Without full financials to back up the statement, it is estimated that SpaceX generated an EBITDA of $8 billion in 2025, though with depreciation and other expenses considered, it is not clear how much (if any) operating (or net) profits the company delivered during the year.

On the funding front, Elon Musk used a portion of his winnings ($180 million) from his PayPal exit as seed money ($100 million) for founding SpaceX, but the company has required multiple rounds of venture capital to fund its infrastructure needs. The first venture capital round of about $12 million was in 2002, but there have been at least thirty additional infusions amounting to more than $12 billion. While SpaceX counts big-name venture capitalists in its investing roster (Founders Fund, Andreessen Horowitz and Sequoia), it has also seen increasing investments from public equity investors such as Fidelity and public tech companies such as Google. While these venture capital investments have diluted Musk's ownership over the years, he continues to own about 42% of the equity in the company, and with differential voting rights, close to 80% of the total voting rights in the company.

Valuing SpaceX

It is true that intrinsic valuation, at least in its discounted cash flow avatar, is much easier to do at companies that have many years of historical data and peer groups of companies in the same business, and there are some who view one or both as pre-requisites. If you adopt that point of view, it is easy to see why so many view SpaceX as a company that cannot be valued (yet), since you don't have access to even a single year of financials, let alone a long history, and there are no true competitors. In fact, it is likely that even if the financial statements are made public in a prospectus, most will continue to avoid valuing the company, using uncertainty about the future as an excuse. If you define intrinsic value as the value of a business based upon its capacity to generate cash flows in the future, there is nothing in that definition that requires either historical data or peer group information, and statistically, the fact that you face uncertainty or that you are missing information does not imply that you cannot make estimates, just that the estimates will be noisier. I have long argued that you can estimate the value of young companies with minimal data, as long as you build a valuation around a business narrative and accept that this valuation will change as circumstances do, and with SpaceX, I will get a chance to put this argument into practice.

To value SpaceX, I consider each of its three core businesses separately since they differ not just on operating metrics but also on the competition faced in each one.

  • The launch business, which is where SpaceX was born, is still its most identifiable business and frames the company's story not just as a futuristic company, but as one that was able to overcome some of the most significant technological challenges of any startup and not just survive but thrive. SpaceX has established such a robust and long-standing cost advantage over its competitors in the business, stemming from its existing infrastructure investments and reusable rocket technology, that it had a market share in excess of 80% of the launch market in 2025. Its competition will come from some private and government-funded players, who may be able to capture market share, notwithstanding their higher costs, due to security and nationalistic concerns.
    • Valuation narrative: The space launch market is estimated to be about $30 billion in 2026 and is expected to grow to $100 billion in 2036, as government and private business demand increases. SpaceX will continue to dominate the business, albeit with a slightly less dominant market share (70%, down from >80% in 2025) of the total market, and as costs decrease with scale, operating margins will increase over time to 40%. (I am being conservative in my estimates, insofar as I am ignoring space travel and expanded business opportunities in space, but I don't think, at the moment, that either offers a viable path to augmenting revenues).
  • The internet service business, built around Starlink (STRLK), is the business that accounts for almost two-thirds of the revenues of the company in 2025, and it builds on the infrastructure built for the launch business, since SpaceX has used it to launch thousands of satellites into space. At the end of 2025, Starlink had close to 10,000 satellites in space, about two-thirds of the entire global count, and is adding to that number every month. That has allowed it to double its subscriber numbers to just over 10 million in the last year, and while Amazon's acquisition of GlobalStar has brought a potential competitor into the mix, GlobalStar has a fraction of the satellites that Starlink does. The challenge for any satellite-based internet service provider is that, notwithstanding the use of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites to improve service, the broadband service lags more conventional internet technology (fiber optic and cable) in much of the world, leaving it (at least for the moment) with a niche market of rural areas, countries with damaged or no infrastructure and people on the go (airplanes, trains and cars). Thus, while the total internet service market is estimated to be close to a trillion and a half dollars globally, in 2025, satellite-based service accounted for about 1% of that market, delivering under $15 billion in revenues, with StarLink having a dominant market share.
    • Valuation narrative: The satellite internet services market will continue to grow, as technology improves service quality and transit demand for better wifi grows, from $15 billion to $160 billion (from less than 1% to 10% of the internet service market) over the next decade. (Starlink will see more competition, but its lead in satellites and capacity to use its launch business to get more into space will give it a substantial advantage and a market share of 75% of the overall market). The cost of customer acquisition will ease over time as business customers become a larger portion of the business, and operating margins will approach 60% in steady state, as the unit economics are very positive.
  • The Large Language Model (LLM) business, from the acquisition of xAI, has brought AI into the SpaceX story, and while that may add to the pricing excitement, Grok lags the other LLMs in terms of revenues and reach, for the moment. In terms of usage, Anthropic (ANTHRO) (with Claude), OpenAI (OPENAI) (with ChatGPT) and Google (GOOGL) (with Gemini) are not only more widely used than Grok in business settings, but are further along in converting them into revenues. While Grok has been bundled into the X Premium subscriptions and earned about $80 million in revenues in 2025, it seems to be focusing more on consumers and only on niche portions of the business market. This is the most diffuse and volatile of the three markets in terms of potential market, since the potential market can run from the tens of billions (if they remain subscription-based) to hundreds of billions or even trillions (if they become replacements for human labor or massive productivity boosters). It is possible that Grok may concede the larger and more competitive business space to Claude and ChatGPT and focus instead on consumer subscriptions, giving it a smaller market but one with less competition.
    • Valuation narrative: The overall LLM market will continue to grow, but more in business applications than in consumer apps, with the market size being determined by how well AI can replicate human labor and regulatory restrictions. xAI will target primarily consumer subscription revenues and niche business applications. That will give it smaller revenues ($80 billion in 2036) than its LLM competitors, but one with less competition and higher margins (50% operating margin) and less reinvestment as it avoids going head-to-head with Anthropic, OpenAI and Google for business use.
  • It is undeniable that SpaceX, as a lead player in three fast-growing and volatile businesses, may be able to use its infrastructure to expand each of these businesses. The space launch business, which has generally focused on delivering commercial or government loads and satellites into outer space, may become a springboard for space travel for leisure, research, or business. With its satellite broadband offerings, the possibility exists that the technology and the reach will improve to a point where the service can compete with fiber-optic and cable broadband offerings, perhaps at a much lower cost. With xAI, the possibility that Grok finds a way to outflank its LLM rivals, including Claude, Gemini and ChatGPT, in terms of business offerings may be low, but it does exist. The recent acquisition of Cursor, a young AI company in the coding space, suggests that xAI has not thrown in the towel on business applications. In truth, these are all options that may not be viable at the moment, but if they become viable, could add immense value.
    • Valuation narrative: This part of the story is built on the expansion options that SpaceX has to enter large markets, with low probability and high payoff. In a crude attempt to capture this part of the story, I will attach an expected revenue in 2036 to these other businesses of $50 billion and an operating margin of 30%. Since these expansion options, if they do exist, will not show up in the near future, the revenues from these options ramp up after year 6 (2032) in the valuation.

Pulling these storylines together as valuation inputs, I estimate the following numbers for 2036 for the three business lines and for the expansion options category:

With these numbers in place, and using a cost of capital reflective of SpaceX's business mix (of aerospace/defense, telecom services and AI) of 8%, we can estimate the company's value:

With my story and inputs, the value that I derive is $1.22 trillion, about 10% below the private market pricing and about a third below the expected IPO pricing, but still astonishingly high for a company with $15.5 billion in revenues in the most recent year and a host of question marks about corporate governance. Note that in this iteration, I have ignored cash and debt, since I do not have the company's financial statements, but it is unlikely that either will have much of an impact on the value of equity for a company with this high a value for its operating assets.

As you can see, the SpaceX story not only has many moving parts but is fraught with uncertainty, and without full financials, it does not have a good starting point. That said, as the story plays out, we will get more clarity, and the story will need to be reworked, with the value consequences unclear. For the moment, though, I am uncertain about every input in my SpaceX valuation, but uncertainty is a continuum, and I am less uncertain about some inputs (such as the revenues and margins in the space launch and satellite internet service businesses) than about other inputs (including the revenues and margins of the LLM and expansion businesses). If you are wondering why the cost of capital is only 8% for the company, close to the median cost of capital for a US company, it is because much of the risk here is specific to the company (thus reducing the effect in a diversified portfolio) and cuts in both directions (upside and downside). In fact, if there are outliers, they are more likely to be on the upside than the downside. With these considerations in mind, I tried to be open about how uncertain I feel about my estimates, and the results of a simulation yield the following distribution for value:

Since the simulation is centered on the same expected values for inputs as I used in my base case, it should come as no surprise that the median value, across ten thousand simulations, of $1.29 trillion is close to the base case valuation of $1.22 trillion. As the pricing for the IPO starts to gain traction, it is worth recognizing that a $1.75 trillion or even a $2 trillion pricing falls in the range of the distribution, though with little or no upside left for an investor paying that price.

If you feel that it is best to wait for the prospectus to be filed before doing the valuation, I understand, but there are three points worth remembering. First, it is unlikely that the prospectus will contain data that will move the intrinsic value story, since none of the numbers in the reported statements will be large enough to alter the immense value coming from expectations of future growth. Second, while the prospectus will contain estimates of total addressable market and perhaps even profitability, in my experience, it will be hype; expect to see trillions of dollars thrown around nonchalantly for market size. Third, as I see it, it is not an either/or proposition, since I can value the company now and revisit the valuation when the prospectus comes out, with the advantage being that you are less likely to be swayed by the sales pitch in the prospectus.

Pricing SpaceX

In an initial public offering, companies are priced, not valued, by bankers (for the offering price), by investors (as the stock starts trading) and by observers to make judgments (on whether it is over- or underpriced). Thus, you can make the argument that the valuation, with all of its moving parts, is irrelevant and that you should price SpaceX, not value it, if your intent is to trade on the IPO. That is a legitimate critique, but the argument that pricing somehow dispenses with the need to make assumptions about market size and profitability or does not have the same uncertainties is not. You can take issue with the intrinsic valuation because of the layers of assumptions that I had to make along the way, and I know that for many investors, either invested already in the company or planning to invest in it, a pricing may seem less daunting. While I sympathize, I am afraid that the uncertainty will be just as much of an issue in pricing SpaceX, and to see why, take a look at the steps in the pricing process:

At each step in the process, you will run into issues.

  • On the pricing metric front, the problem with picking a metric is an information vacuum, with only two scalars, revenues and EBITDA, available, and even if you consider the most recent private market pricing, which priced the company at about $1.25 trillion, as the market value of equity, the absence of debt and cash numbers makes it impossible to back into enterprise value. This problem should be resolved, for the most part, when the company files a full prospectus, but for the moment, if you assume that the net debt number is close to zero, the resulting enterprise value of $1.25 trillion yields nosebleed multiples of 81 times revenues and 156 times EBITDA for the company, using 2025 numbers. Even with minimalist information, there will be pricing variants that use expected revenues (or EBITDA) in a future year as a scalar, as can be seen in this graph:

There is no inherent problem with using forward numbers in pricing, as long as you do the same for all of the companies in your peer group, but in the case of SpaceX. it is inevitable that bullish analysts, unable to justify the sky high pricing values with trailing 12-month numbers, will resort to using forward pricing, and add to the bias, by inflating revenues in future years. In the graph, I have used the forecasted revenues in 2030 and 2035, from my intrinsic valuation, and the EV to Sales ratio drops from 80.13 (112.18) to 3.91 (5.47), using the private company (estimated IPO offering) pricing of $1.25 trillion ($1.75 trillion) as the enterprise value.

  • The even bigger challenge in pricing SpaceX will be in the second step, where you have to find comparable firms (or a peer group) to base your pricing on. There is obviously no company out there that is remotely similar to SpaceX as a composite company, and even if you break it down into businesses, it is likely that you will hit roadblocks. On the space launch business, using publicly traded aerospace and defense companies like Boeing and Northrop Grumman is a non-starter because they are low growth, lower-margin businesses, unlike SpaceX. Palantir may seem like a logical alternative, and while it may have high growth potential, it is a data/software company that does not have the infrastructure needs that SpaceX does. On the internet service business again, there are conventional telecom firms like Verizon and T-Mobile, but the economics of their offerings are different, and they are not growth companies. In April 2026, I computed the multiples of revenues and EBITDA that publicly traded companies in the aerospace/defense, internet services businesses and technology companies trade at, and they are far lower than what SpaceX can be expected to trade at if it goes public at $1.5 to $2 trillion:

    Finally, on xAI, there are other LLMs, almost all of which are private companies now, and while you can use the pricing from most recent VC rounds, you are on shaky ground. Even if you forged ahead with a peer group of high-growth tech companies, your pricing will almost certainly have to revolve around revenues rather than profits and based upon very small samples.

  • The one part of pricing that you will almost certainly see is the storytelling, especially with analysts who are locked into finding SpaceX to be a buy, with the pricing more of an ex-post rationalization than an analytical tool. No matter what peer group you pick, SpaceX will be priced higher than comparable firms, and to back the argument that it is still a good investment, you will hear stories of its large potential market, significant competitive advantages and profitability. All of these stories are grounded in truth, but they are empty if they remain stories. I will predict that there will be far more buy than sell or hold recommendations for SpaceX when it does go public, with analysts doing pricing gymnastics with forward multiples, hand-picked peer groups and fairy tales to justify their positions.

Pricing is an exercise in data analysis, and any pricing of SpaceX will reflect the statistical limitations of the data. Put simply, the only difference between intrinsic valuation and pricing, when it comes to the uncertainties you face with SpaceX, is that with the former (intrinsic valuation), you have to face up to the uncertainties and make your best explicit judgments (on revenues, margins and reinvestment), whereas with the latter (pricing), they remain implicit. If bias is your biggest adversary in assessing SpaceX, and you use pricing, it is very likely that you will find a pricing metric and hand-picked peer group to reflect your biases and then tell yourself a story on why SpaceX is cheap or expensive.

The Bottom Line

SpaceX is an engineering marvel that has shown its naysayers, which included almost every luminary in the space community, to be wrong. That said, for potential investors, there are lessons to be learned from watching Musk's stewardship of Tesla. As with all of Musk's creations, SpaceX will be a shape shifting entity, frustrating investors who expect companies to follow linear paths in the corporate life cycle, going from young growth to maturity; it will shift from one narrative to another, often with no advance warning, causing whiplash for investors. When I bought Tesla in 2019, after its stock had taken a beating, I described the company as my corporate teenager, and with Musk in full control, SpaceX is likely to follow the same unpredictable path. That makes it a difficult company to buy, but it makes it an even more dangerous company to sell short, as Tesla short sellers have discovered in the last two decades. With all that said, SpaceX is a unique company with immense competitive advantages, and while I would not be interested in buying at the rumored IPO pricing of $1.75 trillion, it is one big correction away from being fairly priced or even cheap. If that happens, I will be a buyer but will do so with the recognition that this company comes packaged with a founder who is both uniquely gifted and deeply flawed, and complaining about the parts of Musk you do not like while enjoying the fruits of the aspects that you do is unfair.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ICSH: Simple Cash ETF, Consistent Positive Spread To T-Bills, Solid Income
data.description:
A reader asked for my thoughts on the iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH). ICSH invests in ultra-short-duration bonds with strong credit quality and a duration of only 0.6 years. It is closer to T-bills than to bonds, with a marginal increase in volatility and a slight increase in dividends, with a 4.4% TTM dividend yield and a 4.1% yield to maturity. ICSH is a buy and should be of particular interest to more risk-averse investors looking for cash or cash plus ETFs.

ICSH - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

ICSH is an actively-managed ETF focusing on ultra-short-term investment-grade bonds and securities. It is explicitly not a money market fund, but it's closer to that than to more traditional bond ETFs, as evidenced by its portfolio.

Considering the above, ICSH's characteristics, fundamentals, and performance should all be quite close to those of T-bills. Specifically, expect little in credit or rate risk, overall volatility, and for dividend yields to (roughly) track Federal Reserve rates.

Dividend Yield and Performance

ICSH's investments should trade at a slight positive spread to T-bills simply because these are the safest, highest-quality assets in the world, and everything else gets priced at a spread to these, at least everything denominated in dollars.

ICSH currently trades with a 4.4% dividend yield, higher than the 4.0% dividend yield of the State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL), the largest T-bills ETF in the market. Slightly wider spreads for SEC yields: 4.1% for ICSH and 3.5% for BIL, comparable to yield to maturities of 4.1% for ICSH and 3.7% for BIL. Results here are as expected, with the small differences in spreads being likely due to standard ETF dividend volatility.

ICSH almost always trades at a positive spread to BIL, as expected. Exceptions are few and far between, sometimes due to timing, sometimes due to standard dividend volatility.

Due to the above, ICSH should outperform T-bills long-term, as has been the case since inception. I would expect outperformance to continue, as ICSH trades with a positive spread to T-bills right now, and as these spreads are due to structural market issues, they are likely to persist into the future.

As a quick aside, the noticeable drop in price above occurred because the fund temporarily traded at a discount to NAV during the pandemic. It is rare for an ETF to trade with a discount, although this does sometimes happen during periods of significant market stress, like the pandemic. Lots of ETFs traded at discounts; these were almost always short-lived, so I don't find them to be terribly relevant.

Credit and Rate Risk

ICSH's credit risk is quite low on account of the fund's strong credit quality:

And on account of the fund's low maturity investments.

Specifically, companies with investment-grade ratings rarely go bankrupt or enter into default, and when they do, they generally take more than 9-10 months, the average maturity of ICSH's portfolio. Still, I think there is a small amount of credit risk here, not much, far from significant, but a small amount, definitely more credit risk than T-bills or T-bill ETFs.

I'm not sure that the risk here is significant enough to ensure losses during downturns or recessions, but it seems possible, definitely likelier than for T-bills and the like. ICSH saw a small, short-lived, single-digit drawdown during early 2020, the onset of the coronavirus pandemic. Losses were in the 0.8%-1.5% range, so barely significant. Still, there were losses, and these were higher than for T-bills, which simply didn't see any losses.

Rate Risk and Duration

ICSH focuses on short-term and variable-rate securities, with an average duration of 0.6 years. As with the fund's credit risk, rate risk is extremely low, albeit slightly higher than that of T-bills. The difference in duration is small enough that I'm not sure that losses are guaranteed during a period of rising rates or gains during a period of declining rates. Any price movement would be very low regardless.

ICSH saw a drawdown of less than 1.0% from 2022 to 2023, during which rates skyrocketed. It took the fund less than a year to recover from these, a bit over a year for performance to be functionally identical to that of T-bills, and closer to two to surpass these. At least some of these losses were due to wider credit spreads, and at least some of the underperformance was due to the fact that it takes the fund a bit longer to benefit from higher rates (T-bills would be rolled over in around 2 months on average, ICSH's underlying holdings around 7 months).

Overall Assessment

Overall, ICSH has a broadly similar risk-return profile to that of T-bills, with a slight increase in risk and volatility, a slight increase in dividend yield of around 0.4% as of the moment, and slightly higher realized and expected returns. Looking at the fund's performance these past five years, I think it showcases the situation quite clearly:

In my opinion, as Federal Reserve rates remain at reasonable levels, cash and cash ETFs remain good investments, and that includes ICSH. At the same time, ICSH's overall risk-return profile seems quite good relative to T-bills: both dividend yields and volatility are a bit higher, but the difference in risk is barely noticeable and seems less impactful than the difference in yields.

Finally, ICSH's strategy and portfolio all but ensure a consistent positive spread relative to T-bills. Said spread is a straightforward benefit for investors, one that is almost certain to persist into the future, and one that is likely to lead to higher returns than those of T-bills moving forward. Do bear in mind that dividends might decline due to Federal Reserve rate cuts or other market movements, but a small spread to T-bills should still persist.

Quick Peer Comparison

I cover several strong cash ETFs, so I thought to do a brief comparison between these and ICSH.

Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX)

One of my top cash and income ETFs is the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF. BOXX is a synthetic T-bills ETF, using options to achieve returns extremely similar to those of T-bills, or at least aiming to do so. BOXX's option strategy rarely generates taxable income, which means (close to) zero distributions or taxable events. BOXX's investors are generally able to defer any and all taxes until a moment of their choosing. Income should be taxed at capital gain rates, which are generally lower than income tax rates. BOXX's strategy is not guaranteed to work, but it has generally worked in the past.

Compared to BOXX, ICSH has slightly higher realized and expected returns.

Compared to BOXX, ICSH has a completely different tax treatment. In some cases, arguably most cases, BOXX provides higher after-tax returns to investors in taxable accounts. As taxes are strongly dependent on the specific circumstances of each individual investor, there are bound to be exceptions.

It is impossible and improper to draw general conclusions in tax-related subjects. Still, BOXX seems like the more interesting investment for investors in taxable accounts, and ICSH for those in tax-advantaged accounts.

Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA)

The Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF invests in AAA-rated CLOs. Simplifying things a ton, JAAA indirectly invests in senior secured variable rate loans and receives income for doing so.

Compared to JAAA, ICSH has slightly higher credit risk, as JAAA focuses on AAA-rated CLOs quite strongly, with ICSH leaning more towards those rated A. Rate risk is higher too, with JAAA's CLOs being variable-rate investments with negligible duration, and ICSH having a duration of 0.6 years.

Due to the above, ICSH should be the riskier, more volatile choice. The opposite is true, with JAAA having slightly higher realized volatility and drawdowns. Neither is a particularly risky fund, though, with both figures being low for both ETFs.

AAA-rated CLOs consistently trade at a spread to T-bills, as does JAAA. The same is true of JAAA versus ICSH.

Due to the above, JAAA should outperform ICSH long-term, as has been the case since inception.

As an aside, I'm not entirely sure that AAA-rated investments have to yield more than ICSH's investments, but that is definitely true right now, which implies outperformance.

In my opinion, JAAA is an overall stronger investment opportunity than ICSH. As the former is quite a bit riskier than the latter, ICSH might be of greater interest to more risk-averse investors. In my opinion, JAAA is too risky to be considered a cash ETF, although something like cash plus or ultra-short-term seems reasonable. Some investors might agree and might think JAAA is too risky for their desires (presumably a cash ETF with a stable share price).

Conclusion

ICSH is an ultra-short-term bond ETF. It is quite similar to a T-bills ETF, with a slightly higher dividend yield, slightly greater credit and rate risk, and volatility. In my opinion, and considering prevailing interest rates and ICSH's overall risk-return profile, the fund is a strong investment opportunity and a buy.

data.title: Intel Crushed Q1 Earnings: I'm Heading For The Exit
data.description:
Over the last few quarters, I have gone back and forth on Intel (INTC) more than I expected. Late last year, I was bullish on the stock, and I was exposed to Intel through my position in the SOXL.

That changed drastically after I rotated out of tech in January this year. In fact, in my last update (March 30), I still didn't own the stock, as I have never viewed Intel as a clean turnaround story.

I was lucky enough to start a small position earlier in April, as I quickly noticed that the market was willing to look past the Middle East war noise.

That position is now up 40%, and accounts for about 2% of my NAV. Needless to say, I am trimming my position after the epic jump post Q1 2026 earnings.

In this piece, I discuss my view on the turnaround in the fundamental story of the company after the blowout Q1 earnings. That said, I do not want to let a better fundamental story push me into ignoring a stretched stock. After a 20% post earnings move, a 300% rally since last August, and a valuation that already discounts a lot of improvement, I think the near-term risk-reward has become less attractive. I reiterate my hold rating, and I may reassess if the stock pulls back to the mid-$60s.

A Shift In Perspective After The Q1 2026 Earnings Print

Let's first clarify why Intel is up 19.9% (call it 20%) in the after-hours session.

On Q1 results, the company obliterated the Street's expectations. Revenue was $13.58B, versus about $12.42B expected. On the bottom line, adjusted EPS came in at $0.29 versus roughly $0.01-$0.02 expected.

The spotlight was on the Data Center and AI segment. The Street was looking for $4.41B, and actuals came in at $5.1B, despite the fact that Q1 revenue would have been meaningfully higher if supply had met demand.

As I said in my last piece, I haven't seen Intel being constrained by supply in many years. The problem that I was seeing was demand, given the high competition with AMD's EPYC CPUs and the disappointing performance of its GPUs.

As seen above, the DCAI segment was up 22%, by far driving the 7% total growth of the company last quarter.

On the bottom line, DCAI's operating profit was $1.5 billion, equal to 31% of revenue, up about $292 million sequentially. Again, I think this was the main driver of the EPS beat.

Looking ahead, management said its server CPU demand outlook improved over the last 90 days and now expects a strong year of double digit unit growth for the industry (not surprised here) and for Intel (I'm definitely surprised here), with momentum extending into 2027.

The product to keep a close eye on is the Xeon 6, which (to my surprise) was selected as the host CPU for Nvidia’s DGX Rubin NVL8 systems. In fact, during the quarter, the DCAI business signed multiple long-term agreements in Q1, including none other than Google. I include the excerpt below from the earnings call, as I found it difficult to believe, given that my initial expectation was that Intel's CPUs were used by enterprises and smaller neocloud players, in conjunction with older GPUs:

Within the quarter, DCAI signed multiple long-term agreements, including Google, supporting our view that the current business momentum is sustainable. In addition, Xeon 6 was selected as the host CPU for NVIDIA’s DGX Rubin NVL8 systems, and Xeon remains the most deployed host CPU due to its industry leading memory, security, and networking orchestration. Lastly, DCAI also established a multi-year collaboration with SambaNova to design a next generation, heterogeneous AI inference architecture combining SambaNova’s RDUs, and Intel® Xeon® 6 processors.

The above is the proof that Intel's CPUs (which, by the way, are my favorites for consumer devices) are getting traction with the big names in AI. In my view, this change of perspective is what mainly drove the rally.

Well, there was another factor. Guidance.

For the next quarter, the company guided Q2 revenue to $13.8B-$14.8B, versus $13.07B consensus. On the bottom line, the Q2 non-GAAP EPS guide was $0.20, versus about $0.09 expected.

In other words, the forward guidance told investors that the Q1 strength was not a one-quarter fluke.

Before I conclude the section, I wanted to emphasize a very important point. As I'm writing, I see no upward revision in the analyst consensus.

In fact, as you can see above, the analyst consensus (sales in the chart above) has been on a downtrend since early 2024. In my experience, it would take a few days until analysts digest the results and revise their models. Therefore, I expect a jump on the right side of the chart above, the first meaningful upward revision in multiple years.

Stretched Price Action And Valuation

Let's put things into perspective, right?

Since the U.S. government made the infamous investment in Intel in August last year, shares have been up about 300%.

That move happened in less than 10 months.

Needless to say, after a 20% post earnings jump, I think the gap will get backfilled in the next weeks. That's why jumping in and buying right now has limited upside. On top of that, the stock is trading at 118x forward earnings (that may change as the analyst consensus for the next year's earnings is updated), which makes me wonder how many funds will take profits today, after the market opens.

I know one thing for sure. I'm definitely trimming my position, which is up 40% and accounts for about 2% of my net liquidity value.

Price action tells me the mid-$60s could provide decent support on a pullback. At that level, I may reconsider jumping back into this stock.

Looking Ahead

In my view, Q1 proved the turnaround can show up in revenue, margin, and earnings upside. The company obliterated the consensus in terms of results and guidance, and even gross margins came in 650 bps above the consensus, sitting at 41%.

The blowout quarter tells me that the supply shortage is actually beneficial to the company from a pricing perspective. That's why I was really confused by the market's negative reaction post Q4 2025 earnings.

I am not adding to my position, as I believe there will be some profit-taking in the near term after the post-earnings jump. In fact, I am trimming my position today.

data.title: First American Financial: Strong Earnings Power Ahead Of A Housing Recovery
data.description:
There are stocks that look expensive because the market believes in their story, and then there are stocks that look cheap because the market is looking the other way. First American Financial Corporation (FAF), which I last covered in October 2023, sits firmly in the second category today.

With housing activity still subdued and mortgage rates stubbornly elevated, sentiment around the title insurance industry remains muted. But beneath that surface, First American is building operating leverage, investing in technology, and positioning itself for a powerful earnings inflection when the cycle turns.

This is not just a recovery story. It is a combination of cyclical upside, structural efficiency gains, and a surprisingly durable earnings base that the market may be underestimating.

The Business: More Than Just Title Insurance

First American is best known as one of the largest providers of title insurance and settlement services in the United States. At its core, the company ensures that property ownership is legitimate and that transactions close smoothly. That makes it deeply tied to real estate transaction volumes, both residential and commercial.

But reducing First American to a pure housing proxy misses key parts of the story. The company has built a broader ecosystem that includes data solutions and a growing banking platform through First American Trust. It also has technology initiatives designed to modernize how real estate transactions are executed.

Recent developments highlight that evolution. Management has leaned heavily into AI and automation, rolling out internal platforms that reduce processing times and expand quality control capacity.

At the same time, strategic initiatives like Endpoint aim to digitize and standardize the closing process across its network. These are not incremental tweaks. They are foundational changes that could reshape cost structure and scalability over time.

Earnings Strength: Cyclical Headwinds, Operational Tailwinds

First American’s latest Q1'26 earnings showed just how much progress is happening beneath the surface. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.33, up 58% YoY, a striking result given the still-muted housing backdrop.

The biggest driver was commercial strength. Revenue in that segment surged 48%, with record first-quarter performance. Large transactions are coming back, and the company is benefiting from strong activity across asset classes. Notably, data centers emerged as a major tailwind, with DC-related revenue up 76%. Energy-related transactions also surged, growing 250% and becoming a top-five asset class for the quarter.

Residential, by contrast, remains weak. Purchase revenue declined 4% from the prior year, validating management’s continued cautious stance on the housing market. Refinance activity provided only a temporary boost when mortgage rates dipped, and volumes have since softened again.

What stands out is how well the company performed despite those residential headwinds. That speaks to diversification within commercial and the growing contribution from its banking segment.

Notably, First American Trust generated steady investment income, supported by deposit growth of 19% YoY to $6.8 billion. Importantly, nearly a third of those deposits now come from outside the company’s traditional title business, signaling a more durable and less cyclical funding base.

Forward Outlook: Positioned for a Multi-Layered Recovery

Looking ahead, the investment case hinges on both cyclical recovery and structural growth. On the cyclical side, the housing market does not need to fully normalize for First American to benefit. Even a modest increase in transaction volumes, particularly in residential purchase activity, would provide meaningful operating leverage given the company’s cost structure.

Industry trends support that eventual recovery. Demographic demand remains strong, with millennials continuing to move into prime homebuying years. At the same time, supply constraints have limited transaction volumes rather than underlying demand. When mortgage rates stabilize or decline, activity could rebound quickly.

Commercial real estate adds another layer. While some sectors remain challenged, areas like data centers and energy infrastructure are seeing robust investment. First American’s recent results show it is well positioned to capture that activity.

The banking segment also provides a steady tailwind. As deposit sources diversify and expand, the contribution from investment income should grow, smoothing earnings across cycles.

One of the more underappreciated drivers of future earnings is the company’s aggressive push into AI and workflow automation.

Management highlighted several tangible benefits already in place. AI-assisted examination tools are reducing processing time by roughly 30 minutes per file. In the agency division, automation has expanded quality control capacity more than sixfold.

Endpoint, the company’s digital closing platform, is another key piece. Early pilots show roughly 30% of closing tasks can already be automated. As adoption expands across the branch network, management expects meaningful gains in both efficiency and customer experience.

These improvements matter because title insurance has historically been a labor-intensive business. If First American can structurally reduce time per transaction while maintaining accuracy, the incremental margin on higher volumes could be significant when the market recovers.

Financial Position, Dividends, and Valuation

First American operates from a position of financial strength. It has a BBB- rated balance sheet that’s supported by a safe 32% debt-to-capital ratio.

Capital allocation has remained disciplined. The company has historically returned capital through dividends and share repurchases while continuing to invest in strategic initiatives. As shown below, FAF opportunistically bought back shares a few years ago when shares were much cheaper.

FAF currently yields a respectable 3.2%. The dividend comes with a 5-year CAGR of 4% and 15 consecutive years of growth. I continue to see value in the stock at the current price of $68.84 with a forward P/E of 10.6, sitting well under its normal P/E of 14.9, as shown below.

Given its robust momentum across sectors and analyst estimates for 7-9% EPS growth annually over the next two years, investors could see double-digit total returns. This is when also factoring in dividends and reversion to mean valuation.

Risks to Consider

The most immediate risk is the housing market itself. If mortgage rates remain elevated for longer than expected, residential transaction volumes could stay depressed, limiting near-term earnings growth.

Commercial real estate also carries uncertainty. While some sectors are sound, others, particularly office, remain under pressure. A broader slowdown in commercial transactions would weigh on results.

Finally, title insurance is a competitive industry with pricing pressure during downturns. Maintaining margins in a low-volume environment requires careful cost control and operational discipline.

Investor Takeaway

First American Financial is not a flashy story, and that is precisely what makes it interesting. The market is focused on current housing weakness, but the company is building a stronger, more scalable business underneath.

Earnings are already showing resilience, driven by commercial strength, banking contributions, and early benefits from technology investments. When residential activity eventually recovers, those same factors could amplify the upside.

At today’s valuation, investors are effectively paying for a subdued housing environment. Meanwhile, they are getting the potential for margin expansion, deposit-driven earnings stability, and a cyclical rebound as a free option. As such, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on FAF.

data.title: Get Out Of Super Micro Computer While You Can
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is down over 11.5% on Thursday renewed negative headlines.

We're seeing emerging reports around the company's contract with Oracle (ORCL) for Nvidia’s (NVDA) GB300 NVL72 racks. According to Bluefin research, Oracle canceled a contract estimated at $1.1 billion to $1.4 billion following the indictment in late March. Bluefin research also raised concerns around the B200 inventory that SMCI has, and called it “considerable,” with demand shifting to GB200 NVL72 and racks awarded to competitors. The concern that arises here is that it may grow increasingly difficult for SMCI to get this inventory off its hands, considering that in the second half of the year, Nvidia’s Vera Rubin will be everyone's focus.

Before I get into the exhaustive details, when I last covered the stock following the scandal, shedding over 33% in a single day, I saw a buying opportunity. As a matter of fact, I upgraded the stock to a strong buy as I saw that the negatives were priced in at the time.

With the stock reaching the $29 level on Wednesday, my expectations have played out, even better than expected. The stock is now up over 30% since my strong buy, and I’m downgrading it to a sell until further notice, especially given the renewed uncertainty the report introduces.

My longer-term thesis remains, but I’m also not underestimating the market’s tendency to punish this name twice for the same mistake, especially when the second leg of the story introduces structural cost increases, and uncertainty around customers.

Why the risk-reward turned unfavourable:

The original SMCI sell-off treated the Liaw indictment as an event, something that got quantified and could be digested. A single-day 33% drop is the market's way of saying let's move on, and that's exactly the logic I followed when I upgraded to strong buy.

But if you layer the news from Thursday on top of SMCI's existing credibility deficit, including the Hindenburg report, the EY resignation, the delayed 10-K, the Nasdaq compliance scare, the ongoing DOJ interest, what you have is a governance overhang, which will show up on the numbers with Oracle’s cancellation. Every time the market was ready to let the latter’s discount compress, something new has emerged to justify it.

My original thesis, which is that fundamentals and AI server demand would overwhelm the scandal impact, has largely played out in the 30%+ move since my upgrade, but the risk-reward scenario from current levels looks increasingly unfavourable.

Fundamental risks:

I'm not saying SMCI is a broken story, and I want to make that clear. The AI server TAM is enormous, and SMCI's engineering capability is real. When the risk-reward was skewed positive after an overdone sell-off, I took the trade. I’m recommending investors take the profit and wait.

Market share risks are also a point of concern. On the latter, one must also consider competition. Back in 2024, according to estimates, the company had 40% to 50% market share in the AI GPU server deployments, down from around 80% in 2022.

SMCI previously held around 7.4% of global server OEM sales and now sits closer to 4%, nearly halving its footprint in the broader server market at the exact moment the company is being marketed as an AI infrastructure leader.

The top five server companies collectively saw their combined share decline from 33.3% to 22.6%, as Asian OEMs like undercut Western pricing with lower-cost manufacturing footprints, while hyperscalers simultaneously push internal silicon programs like Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS Trainium and Inferentia, Google's (GOOG) TPU, and Microsoft's (MSFT) Maia to reduce external OEM dependence over time. SMCI is the second-largest OEM seller of AI-aligned servers, but with roughly 63% of quarterly revenue coming from a single large data center customer, and over 90% of revenue tied to AI GPU platforms, the situation is now far from uncomplicated.

SMCI is losing share in the broader OEM market, and depending on one customer for nearly two-thirds of its revenue, all while that customer and its peers are spending serious capital to make SMCI less relevant to their infrastructure stack over the medium term. One other big point of concern is 2Q26 gross margin coming in at 6.4% non-GAAP, down from 9.5% in 1Q and double-digits in the 2024 peak era.

According to the company’s CEO, Charles Liang, on the last earnings call:

the customer mix, we are improving quarter after quarter. Now we have many more large-scale customer, I would like to say. So that will improve our profitability. The other factor is -- last quarter, I mean, December quarter, the GP300 was a little bit new to us. So a lot of expedite transportation cost. And now, I mean, product is getting mature. So those expedite transportation costs will be dramatically reduced and tariff impact also improving. And -- so overall, especially DCBBS also increasing for our -- for our gross margin. So I believe our gross margin will start to improve quarter after quarter.

I think a lot rests on margins, and that’s a risk investors should keep an eye on going forward.

Valuation and what’s next:

The stock is set to open at around $26, down from $29 at Wednesday’s close. If the losses don’t deepen, the stock would still be holding above its EMA21 and EMA50, but still below the $35 level where the EMA200 sits and is acting as resistance. The stock has been on a downward trend since early October of last year, and has seen a handful of good days since, reaching a bottom of $19.4 in late March. While it has recovered since, it is nowhere near the top, and is actually closer to the bottom at these levels. Following the Oracle report, I expect the stock to price in more of the negatives, especially if others follow Oracle’s footsteps, with the latter down around 3% on the news alone.

In comparison to DELL and HPE, SMCI is the cheapest on forward PE, forward EV/Sales, and almost every other forward metric.

While that might look attractive in retrospect, I expect some more weakness to get priced into the stock in the near term, and thus see the exit at current levels as the next best move before getting a better entry point.

data.title: Layoffs Are Accelerating Again
data.description:
It's been a crazy week for layoff announcements. Big Tech firms are continuing to clear out their rosters.

Amazon (AMZN) is closing a warehouse that opened less than two years ago only a few months after having laid off ~30,000 workers; Meta Platforms (META) is cutting its staff by 10%, which translates to ~8,000 employees; and Microsoft (MSFT) is offering buyouts to 7% of its U.S. workforce. This comes after other tech firms like Oracle (ORCL) cut thousands of jobs last month.

It's not just tech firms; Nike (NKE) is laying off ~1,400 workers too. But the workers laid off from Nike were all in technology-related roles in the operations division, so even these are ultimately tech layoffs.

A popular online tracker of tech sector layoff announcements, Layoffs.fyi, is now showing ~92,270 layoffs from tech companies alone so far in 2026. This puts us well ahead of schedule to blow past the 2025 end figure of 124,201, which is alarming in itself. Don't let the global nature of the figures fool you; 2/3rds of those layoffs are from the U.S.

AI Is the Cause and Effect

In early February, I wrote about an alarming print from the Challenger Report, which tracks layoff announcements, which I had written off as being idiosyncratically related to UPS (UPS) and Amazon (AMZN) reducing their transportation divisions. It highlighted that while large companies still don't employ the majority of Americans, they do employ such a large number of people that single cuts can trigger massive movements in absolute terms.

Since January, the layoff figure has moderated, but we'll get an update on this in a week and a half with April's figures, and I expect it to tick back up again due to all of these recent announcements. Challenger noted that tech layoff plans were accelerating, up 40% YoY, led by layoffs at Dell (DELL).

Challenger Report History

I covered what I think the reason is in a recent article titled “AI Already Took 3 Million Jobs,” where I presented a counterfactual for the AI jobs narrative. If we looked at the pre-2019 trend in knowledge worker jobs, where would we be if there was no ChatGPT?

Here's from that article, relevant to why these layoffs are accelerating:

Enter the counterfactual, an index constructed of white-collar sectors at threat from AI tools: finance, insurance, information, professional services, and business services jobs as reported by the census. We can abbreviate it “FIIPB” for our purposes. That group accounts for 40% of GDP and has historically shown a fairly linear growth rate; it was steady from 2015 to 2019 at ~1.9% annual growth. When we extend the trend and look at where we are now, the counterfactual shows us that we're off-trend by ~8%. That's 3 million jobs.

Two-thirds of that loss is in professional and business services, which didn't see the same steepness as information jobs but account for the majority of the off-trend data. This checks out to me—AI replaces much of what you may have been hiring for in these fields, like consultants...

And this is backed up by what's going on in the boardroom. In Q1 this year, CEOs spent a lot of time discussing AI; it was the most emphasized keyword across the 12,000 earnings calls that IoT Analytics tracked. They can't get enough of it, from the topic itself to related keywords like agentic and physical AI, Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT—they all made it too, and even technical AI terms like “MCP” got onto the board for Q1.

While I do suspect that there are corporations using AI as cover to mask other reasons for layoffs that are far less flattering for their image, there is significant reason to believe that there is a trend in job destruction in both future and present jobs in the knowledge sector.

All of this is showing up in unemployment data. While the headline BLS figure is falling, that's been in part due to workers leaving the labor force and becoming “marginally attached.” Broader views of unemployment that include those workers and those underemployed (working part-time but seeking full-time work), like Morning Consult's unemployment index, show that levels are rising and above their YoY levels.

It's Not All Gloomy Labor Data

Despite all of this, I have a somewhat rosy view of the direction of the labor market. I believe that we're over the big labor hump and that these tech layoffs are concentrated among IT workers and that those workers are being absorbed quickly. We're still cooling, and I'm not saying that the labor market is improving. But I believe that we're over the worst of the tight labor conditions.

Initial jobless claims have come in under expectations and well below their YoY figure so far this year. There are ~20,000 fewer starting claims than there were this time last year.

Private sector employment is still growing despite the layoffs. ADP Research ((ADP)) is showing a positive uptick in employment ahead of the lagging BLS data. If ADP is correct in its directionality, this would constitute more confidence than the media headlines are projecting for the labor market.

Since the release of agentic coding tools last summer, we've also seen a structural rise in business application filings. So some job loss is being absorbed by the fact that entrepreneurship comes with lower barriers to entry.

As expected in an AI-fueled entrepreneurship boom, the fastest-growing sector is professional services. This is exactly where AI being able to do knowledge work is useful, in replacing salaried workers in both large and small businesses. That comes with both layoffs and new business ventures.

Will This Move The Fed to Act?

The labor market has been the key reason for the Fed to cut rates this year, and the unemployment rate falling from its peak (alongside the rise in inflation coming from fuel prices), so this mixed outlook rightfully has made them fall back into the “wait and see” mode that they took for much of last year. You can see it in the rate chart; long periods of holding rates go by between sets of rate cuts more.

The Fed is calling this prudence, though other commentators are calling it reactionary and too slow.

The current outlook is that while the labor market remains a mixed bag with specific pockets of pain (young graduates, coders, etc.), inflation is still a concern and over target, so the Fed will be in “wait and see” mode for the foreseeable future.

The market has priced in the Fed holding rates through all of this year and next, with the first cut currently projected to come in December 2027.

I think that this is rather dramatic and that the odds are likely to change quickly if new data hits of a worsening of labor market conditions. Not only am I still expecting the unemployment rate to moderate and level off soon, I expect that to motivate the Fed, once the energy shock has worked its way through in the coming months, to cut rates again toward the end of this year.

A lot can happen between now and then. My September timeline is absolutely in jeopardy, and traders are currently pricing in an 80% chance of rates being held at the December meeting this year. I'd bet otherwise, and I'd bet on one cut lower by then.

Conclusion

Recent layoff announcements have raised rightful alarms in labor market watchers, especially those looking at jobs in the tech sector. There is evidence to suggest that AI is replacing knowledge workers, in what CEOs are saying to cover for layoffs and in their earnings calls, as well as in the labor data for knowledge workers at large.

Despite this, I believe that the total picture of the labor market is looking like it is no longer worsening. Headline unemployment is falling, despite marginal attachment rising. But other metrics like initial jobless claims are coming in under expectations and lower than previous years, private payroll projections are showing near-term growth in hiring, and business formation in sectors benefiting from AI (potentially led by those tech workers recently laid off going in on their own projects) are rising faster and looking structurally higher than in the past.

All told, this puts the Fed in a bind for their prior rate cut plans. Their new plan to wait and see, which traders expect to last for the foreseeable future, hinges on the worst of labor market pain being behind us. I share that view, although I expect them to find cover to cut rates sooner than the market consensus, perhaps by the end of this year.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: 2 BDCs To Sell Before It Gets Worse
data.description:
The BDC sector has been hammered hard from multiple sides.

It is the cockroaches, SaaSpocalopyse, capped private BDC redemptions, and several other themes that have all contributed to heavy discounts across the board.

As I have laid out in numerous previous articles, there is a very good chance that all these headlines are just creating noise and seeding unjustified panic.

We could, of course, argue about BDC fundamentals deteriorating on the margin, but not about the current discounts really materializing.

Just take a look at my recent piece, BDCs: The Time To Buy This 14% Yielding Sector Is Now, in which I have contextualized the prevailing discounts to a) most recent BDC financial data on the ground and b) historical credit shock events, which triggered a surge in non-accruals.

The conclusions are clear: at least based on Q4 2025 data points, BDCs show no signs of system-wide deterioration, and, just to put the recent non-accruals in perspective, they would have to increase by ~10x from Q4 levels, and the bankruptcies would have to land at a rate that is double of what we experienced in the COVID-19 period for the sector average P/NAV to become justified.

So, with this kind of backdrop in mind and with an increased bias towards high-quality BDCs, I have entered into a full BDC buying mode. Namely, all my incremental portfolio contributions go to BDCs until their portfolio's current income weight reaches close to 20% or until the discounts are halved (whichever is first).

Having said that, let me once again underscore the second aspect that I mentioned above, i.e., increased bias towards high-quality BDCs.

It is simple. It means two things.
